text: The surgeon performs a subcarinal lymph node dissection, carefully exposing the area while preserving the vagal branches. A dissection plane is created around the right main bronchus using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon grasps the right main bronchus to create space for the harmonic device to operate, focusing on the bronchial and nodal branches.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a clear dissection of nodal branches around the right main bronchus, avoiding the need for clips or additional instruments.
The surgeon performs esophageal dissection and lymph node dissection, aiming to remove potential metastatic tissue from the neck to the diaphragm. Gauze is requested during the procedure.

The surgeon immobilizes the esophagus to prepare for anastomosis, carefully avoiding damage to the nearby nerve. The nerve and esophagus are shown in close proximity, highlighting the importance of cautious manipulation.

The final view shows the surgical field after complete separation of the esophagus and removal of lymph nodes along the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves. The trachea and major airways remain intact, and the left hilum is fully exposed. The left pleura is visibly intact, and the diaphragmatic lymph nodes have been completely excised.
The surgeon shows the prepared stomach in the abdominal cavity, indicating readiness for further surgical steps.
The surgeon widens the hiatus by cutting part of the right crus. The suture site between the resected esophagus and the stomach graft conduit is visible, and the stomach graft is being pulled up.
The surgeon visually assesses the stomach graft for resistance, determining the limit of tension before deciding to stop pulling. The surgeon then prepares to cut the suture at the graft site.

The surgeon identifies the tumor location approximately 27 centimeters from the inside and prepares to cut around this area. The surgeon uses scissors to grasp and cut the tissue, aiming to create an anastomosis at the identified site.
The surgeon checks the positioning of the Levin tube and instructs to pull the L-tube back, ensuring proper placement within the surgical field.
